question.text: Public Provident Fund (PPF) follows which taxation regime under the old tax system?

answer.text: EEE (Exempt-Exempt-Exempt) — contribution is deductible, interest is exempt, and maturity proceeds are exempt
EET (Exempt-Exempt-Taxed) — contribution is deductible, interest is exempt, but maturity proceeds are taxed
ETE (Exempt-Taxed-Exempt) — contribution is deductible, interest is taxed annually, and maturity proceeds are exempt
TEE (Taxed-Exempt-Exempt) — contribution gets no deduction, but interest and maturity are both exempt

explanation.text: **The Logic:** PPF enjoys EEE status under the old tax regime: (1) Contribution qualifies for deduction under Section 80C (first E — Exempt), (2) Interest earned is not taxable (second E — Exempt), (3) Maturity proceeds are fully exempt (third E — Exempt). This makes PPF one of the most tax-efficient instruments available. **The Trap:** Option (d) TEE describes what PPF effectively becomes under the new tax regime, where Section 80C deduction is not available — the contribution is made from post-tax income (Taxed), but interest and maturity remain exempt. EET describes ELSS under the old regime (maturity/redemption gains are taxable as capital gains). ETE describes tax-saving FDs under the old regime. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series V-A MFD exam extensively tests EEE/EET classifications to compare mutual funds with other savings instruments. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When advising clients who have chosen the new regime, highlight that PPF loses its first 'E' — making the effective taxation TEE. Clients should reconsider allocation accordingly.
